structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health record system containing 10,000 patients over 5 years (60 months). Data includes demographic attributes, clinical history (prior admissions, diagnoses, procedures), medication records, vital signs, lab results, and discharge summaries. Readmission outcome labels are extracted from follow-up records and clinical documentation within 30 days of discharge. Data quality: missing values are documented per feature; extreme outliers in vital signs are reviewed with clinical domain experts before imputation decisions; lab results ordered but not resulted are marked as missing rather than imputed.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission to the same hospital system within 30 days of discharge (index admission). Readmission is defined as an inpatient admission (not ED-only visits) with an admission date ≤30 days from discharge date of the index admission. Transfers to other facilities on the same day are excluded. Label is determined from administrative records and is treated as ground truth.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split stratified by readmission status. The cohort is ordered chronologically by discharge date. The first 60% of discharges (chronologically) form the training set (~6,000 patients, discharge window: months 1–36). The next 20% (chronologically, ~2,000 patients, discharge window: months 37–48) form the validation set. The final 20% (~2,000 patients, discharge window: months 49–60) form the held-out test set. Within each set, stratified sampling ensures the positive class (readmission) proportion is preserved. Justification: temporal splitting prevents leakage of future information and respects the real-world deployment scenario where the model must generalize to future patients. Stratification ensures balanced class representation in all three sets, enabling fair evaluation even with class imbalance.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit exclusively on the training set. The fitted transformers are then applied to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents data leakage and ensures the validation and test performance estimates reflect true generalization. Specifically: (1) mean/std scaling for continuous features (vital signs, lab values) is computed from training set; (2) categorical encoding (one-hot or ordinal) is learned from training set categories; (3) missing value imputation (median for continuous, mode for categorical) uses training set statistics; (4) any feature selection or threshold decisions are made on training data only.
structured_choices.features: Raw temporal features: admission date, discharge date, length of stay, number of prior admissions in past 12 months, number of prior admissions in past 30 days (recency), time since last admission. Clinical features: primary diagnosis (ICD-10 code, one-hot encoded), comorbidity count, Charlson comorbidity index, presence of specific high-risk diagnoses (heart failure subtype, renal dysfunction, diabetes). Treatment features: number of medications at discharge, presence of ACE inhibitor/ARB use, diuretic use, beta-blocker use, length of ICU stay (if applicable). Demographic features: age at discharge, sex, insurance type (proxy for SES), rurality (from zip code). Derived features: (1) readmission risk bins from prior admissions (0, 1, 2, 3+ admissions in past year); (2) medication compliance proxy (consistency of medication refills pre-discharge if available); (3) temporal sequence features for the RNN: 12-month prior admission history encoded as a sequence of (admission_date, length_of_stay, primary_diagnosis) tuples, zero-padded to length 10. No feature encodes the label or uses information from post-discharge events.
structured_choices.model: Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM-based) trained with triplet loss. Architecture: embedding layer (input dimension = feature count, embedding dimension = 64), LSTM layer (64 units, 1 layer), dense layer (32 units, ReLU), output layer (3 units for triplet loss: anchor, positive, negative embeddings). Triplet loss formulation: for each patient discharge record (anchor), a positive example is a readmitted patient similar in clinical profile, and a negative example is a non-readmitted patient. During inference, the model outputs a single embedding; a threshold-based classifier (or logistic regression fitted on validation embeddings) produces binary predictions. Training: Adam optimizer, learning rate 1e-3, batch size 32, early stopping on validation loss (patience 10 epochs), max 100 epochs.
structured_choices.baseline: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel. Feature input: the same raw and derived features used for the RNN (excluding the temporal sequence features; instead, the RNN temporal features are reduced to aggregate statistics: mean/max/min of admission dates, total prior admissions). Hyperparameter tuning: grid search over C ∈ [0.1, 1, 10, 100] and gamma ∈ [1e-3, 1e-2, 0.1, 1] performed on the validation set. The tuned SVM is evaluated on the test set. Justification: SVM with RBF kernel is a strong, well-calibrated baseline for binary classification in healthcare; it does not use temporal structure (by design, to isolate the advantage of the RNN's sequential modeling). Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search on validation set) and are evaluated on the same test set under identical conditions.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Justification: readmission rates are typically 10–20% (imbalanced), and the operational goal is to correctly identify high-risk patients for intervention (precision matters). AUPRC is the appropriate summary metric for imbalanced binary classification, as it is insensitive to the proportion of negative examples, unlike AUROC. The hospital's resource allocation strategy benefits from high precision (minimizing false alarms) and good recall (minimizing missed high-risk cases); AUPRC captures this trade-off. Threshold for positive prediction will be selected on the validation set to maximize F1-score, which balances precision and recall for the deployed model.
secondary_metrics: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) — for completeness and comparability to literature
Sensitivity (recall) at 90% specificity — captures the clinical goal of identifying true readmissions while minimizing false alarms
Positive Predictive Value (precision) at the selected operating point — directly reflects the utility of the model for resource allocation
Calibration: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and calibration plots — ensures predicted probabilities are reliable for clinical decision-making
structured_choices.model_selection_data: The validation set (20% of data, chronologically months 37–48) is used to (1) perform hyperparameter tuning (grid search for SVM, early stopping for RNN), and (2) select the decision threshold for binary classification by maximizing F1-score. The validation set is used iteratively during model development but is never used to report final performance. The test set is held out and used exactly once at the end to report primary and secondary metrics.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (20% of data, months 49–60) is locked and not accessed during model development, hyperparameter tuning, or threshold selection. After both the RNN and SVM models are finalized using the training and validation sets, each model is applied to the test set exactly once. Predictions and performance metrics are computed and reported. No retraining, retuning, or threshold adjustment occurs after test set evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Confound: socio-economic status (SES) and access to follow-up care influence readmission risk independently of clinical severity. Controls: (1) insurance type (a proxy for SES) is included as a feature in both models, allowing the model to learn its relationship with readmission while controlling for unmeasured SES effects via stratification; (2) stratified analysis: after final test set evaluation, performance is stratified by insurance type (private, Medicare, Medicaid, uninsured) to check for differential model performance across SES proxies; (3) sensitivity analysis: a separate subgroup evaluation is performed on patients from rural vs. urban settings (rurality is a proxy for follow-up care access); (4) documentation: any substantial performance differences across strata are reported alongside the primary result, with interpretation that the model may not generalize equally to all patient populations. Confound that is NOT controlled: unobserved social factors (e.g., social support, transportation) are not captured in the EHR and are acknowledged as a limitation.

design_narrative:
To test whether an RNN with triplet loss outperforms an SVM at predicting 30-day readmission for heart failure patients, we propose the following experimental design:

**1. Data and Labeling**

We will use the full 10,000-patient EHR dataset spanning 5 years. The outcome label is binary: readmission to the same hospital system within 30 days of discharge (index admission). Transfers on the same day and ED-only visits are excluded. Labels are extracted from administrative records and cross-referenced with clinical documentation. We will document missing data rates per feature and apply clinically-informed imputation only after domain expert review of outlier patterns in vital signs and lab results.

**2. Train-Validation-Test Split**

We will use a temporal split stratified by readmission status. Patients are ordered chronologically by discharge date. The first 60% of discharges (months 1–36) comprise the training set (~6,000 patients). Discharges in months 37–48 form the validation set (~2,000 patients). Discharges in months 49–60 form the held-out test set (~2,000 patients). Within each set, stratification preserves the readmission class proportion. This temporal approach prevents future information leakage and mirrors the real-world setting where the model must generalize to future admissions.

**3. Feature Engineering**

Both models receive the same feature set, constructed from raw EHR signals: (1) temporal features (admission/discharge dates, length of stay, prior admission history aggregated over 12 and 30 days); (2) clinical features (primary diagnosis, Charlson comorbidity index, high-risk condition flags); (3) treatment features (medication classes at discharge); (4) demographics (age, sex, insurance type as an SES proxy, rurality). For the RNN, we also construct a temporal sequence of prior admissions (diagnosis and LOS) over the past 12 months, zero-padded to length 10. All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied without modification to validation and test sets.

**4. Model Architecture and Baseline**

The proposed RNN uses an LSTM with triplet loss: embeddings are learned such that similar readmitted patients are close in embedding space and different patients are far. The architecture includes an embedding layer, one LSTM layer (64 units), a dense layer (32 units, ReLU), and outputs three embeddings for triplet loss. Inference uses a learned threshold applied to the final embedding to produce binary predictions.

The baseline is an SVM with RBF kernel, using the same feature set (temporal features reduced to aggregate statistics, excluding the raw sequence). Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set: the SVM tunes C and gamma; the RNN uses early stopping with patience 10 epochs.

**5. Model Selection and Evaluation**

The validation set is used to select hyperparameters and to tune the decision threshold (maximizing F1-score) for both models. The test set is locked during all development and tuned only once at the end.

The primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), chosen because readmission is imbalanced (~10–20% positive) and the operational goal is to identify high-risk patients for intervention. AUPRC is insensitive to class imbalance and directly reflects precision-recall trade-offs relevant to resource allocation. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for comparison), sensitivity at 90% specificity (a clinically meaningful operating point), precision at the selected threshold, and calibration error (Expected Calibration Error and calibration plots).

**6. Confound Management**

We account for the confound of socio-economic status and follow-up care access by: (1) including insurance type and rurality as features in both models; (2) performing stratified analysis on the test set, reporting performance separately for private, Medicare, Medicaid, and uninsured populations, as well as rural vs. urban; (3) documenting any substantial differential performance as a limitation of generalizability. Unmeasured social factors (e.g., social support) are acknowledged as uncontrolled confounds.

**7. Scope of Claims**

This experiment establishes whether the RNN with triplet loss achieves higher AUPRC than the SVM on this EHR cohort under the specified temporal split. It does not establish causality, does not generalize outside the 5-year window or hospital system, and may not generalize equally to all patient subgroups (as flagged by stratified analysis). Success is defined as superior AUPRC on the held-out test set with maintained or improved secondary metrics and calibration.
